Phase 1: Exploration Phase 2: Preparation Phase 3: Implementation Phase 4: Sustainment

Phase 4: Sustainment

Normalise acupuncture and acupressure into routine hospital practice so the intervention persists beyond the implementation team.

Bottom Line Implementations often fail after the project team moves on. Sustainability depends on normalising practices into routine operations — making them 'just how we do things here.' This requires ongoing attention to both outer factors (external leadership, inter-organisational links, funding) and inner factors (fidelity, staffing, coaching).

Overview

The most common failure mode in hospital innovation is not failed implementation — it is failed sustainment. Programmes that were running well can quietly fade once the initial champions move on, funding cycles end, or staff turn over.

Sustainment depends on normalising the acupuncture/acupressure intervention into routine operations and ensuring it continues to meet organisational needs. This requires attention to both outer (external) and inner (organisational) factors.

Outer Factors

External Leadership

External leadership support — from government bodies, professional associations, or health department directives — can significantly bolster sustainment. When acupuncture/acupressure is endorsed or recommended in external guidelines, it provides institutional legitimacy that outlasts any individual champion.

Actions to strengthen external leadership support:

  • Align your programme with national or state-level clinical guidelines
  • Seek endorsement from relevant professional bodies
  • Publish outcomes to contribute to the broader evidence base
  • Present at conferences to build peer recognition
Links to Other Organisations

When peer hospitals are implementing similar practices, it creates constructive pressure to sustain your own programme. Organisational networks also provide shared learning, troubleshooting support, and collective advocacy.

Strategies:

  • Connect with other hospitals that have implemented acupuncture/acupressure
  • Join or establish an implementation network or community of practice
  • Share outcomes, protocols, and training materials across organisations
  • Participate in collaborative research or multi-site audits
Funding

Long-term funding is a critical determinant of sustainment. The key question is whether acupuncture/acupressure can be demonstrated to be cost-neutral or cost-saving compared to existing practice.

Consider:

  • Is the intervention cost-neutral when reductions in rescue medication and recovery time are factored in?
  • Can consumable costs (wristbands, needles) be absorbed into existing budgets?
  • Are there grant or quality improvement funding streams that support ongoing delivery?
  • Can patient outcome improvements (shorter stays, fewer complications) be translated into a business case for ongoing investment?

PONV example: Acupressure wristbands cost ~$5 AUD per patient. If they reduce rescue antiemetic use (RR 0.62) and contribute to shorter recovery room stays, the intervention may be cost-saving overall.

Inner Factors

Organisational Leadership

Internal leadership support must be cultivated from Phase 1 onward and actively maintained during sustainment. Key strategies:

  • Regular audit reports to leadership — keep the programme visible in organisational reporting. Out of sight is out of mind.
  • Align outcomes with organisational mission — frame acupuncture/acupressure outcomes in terms leadership cares about: patient experience scores, complication rates, length of stay, innovation reputation.
  • Leadership engagement from the start — leaders who were involved in the decision to implement are more likely to sustain support.
  • Succession planning — ensure that when key leaders move on, their successors understand and support the programme.
Fidelity

Fidelity refers to whether the intervention continues to be delivered as intended. Over time, practices can drift — sometimes subtly — reducing effectiveness.

Common Fidelity Issues

  • Wristband displacement below the correct PC6 location
  • Shortened treatment duration as staff become casual about timing
  • Incomplete documentation making outcomes untrackable
  • New staff delivering the intervention without adequate training

Strategies to Maintain Fidelity

  • Onsite monitoring and regular observational audits
  • Staff feedback loops — ask practitioners what difficulties they encounter
  • Visible, accessible education materials at point of care (posters, laminated guides)
  • Regular education sessions aligned with routine practice protocols (e.g., annual competency reviews)
  • Periodic re-assessment using the audit tools from Phase 3
Staffing and Support Coaching

Staff turnover is the greatest threat to sustainment. New staff who were not part of the original implementation may not understand why the intervention exists or how to deliver it correctly.

Strategies

StrategyDetail
Unit-level champions Identify an enthusiastic champion in each work unit who can onboard new staff, answer questions, and maintain momentum.
Manager reminders Include acupuncture/acupressure in regular management communications — team meetings, handover protocols, quality dashboards.
Accessible education materials Keep training resources visible and easy to find. Laminated point-of-care guides, intranet pages, and QR codes linking to video tutorials.
Orientation integration Add acupuncture/acupressure to new staff orientation programmes so it is learned as a standard part of practice from day one.
Ongoing education Schedule regular refresher sessions matching the frequency of other mandatory clinical education (e.g., annual skills updates).

Sustainment Self-Check

Is Your Programme Sustainable? Key Questions

Use these questions for periodic self-assessment:

DomainQuestion
Leadership Does current leadership actively support the programme?
Funding Is the programme funded through a sustainable budget line?
Fidelity Are recent audits showing the intervention is delivered correctly?
Staffing Do new staff receive training as part of orientation?
Documentation Are outcomes still being tracked and reported?
External support Is the intervention included in current clinical guidelines?
Patient experience Are patients still being offered the intervention consistently?

If the answer to any of these questions is "no" or "I don't know," that domain needs attention to prevent programme erosion.

Phase 4 Resources

  • Sustainment self-assessment checklist
  • Fidelity audit template
  • New staff orientation guide template
  • Leadership reporting template
  • Cost-effectiveness business case template
